title: Top Five Movies of 2021 So Far. Plus: Questlove on ‘Summer of Soul’
format: Sean and Amanda run down their top five movies of the year so far, then Sean talks to Questlove about directing Summer of Soul.

films.title: Summer of Soul
films.note: Seen together at Sundance in January. A concert documentary built from previously unseen footage of the 1969 Harlem Cultural Festival, opening on Stevie Wonder's drum solo.

segments.heading: The State of the 2021 Movie Year
segments.note: six months in, uneven so far

films.title: F9
films.note: A little below average for the Fast series, per Sean.

films.title: Godzilla vs. Kong
films.note: Nice to have at that moment, but not top-five material for either franchise.

films.title: Mortal Kombat
films.note: Wanted it to be good. It wasn't.

films.title: The Conjuring: The Devil Made Me Do It
films.note: Not the best Conjuring movie.

films.title: The Human Voice
films.note: Pedro Almodóvar's 30-minute short with Tilda Swinton. It doesn't fit the usual runtime, but Sean says it's as good as almost anything out this year.

films.title: Minari
films.note: Both realized mid-taping they'd forgotten to consider it, having seen it at Sundance 2020, but its wide release and box office run were 2021.

films.title: Luca
films.note: Pixar's, not yet mentioned on the show before this.

films.title: Raya and the Last Dragon
films.note: Long theatrical tail for a movie that's been out for months.

films.title: Independence Day
films.note: Nostalgia detour: both were pre-teens when it hit theaters, and it brought back memories of packed-house screaming.

segments.heading: Sean Fennessey's Top Five Movies of 2021 So Far
segments.subject: Movies of 2021

films.title: Judas and the Black Messiah
films.note: No. 1. Sean's been turning over the pushback since release. He thinks it holds together as a cat-and-mouse thriller rather than a straight Fred Hampton biopic.

films.title: Summer of Soul
films.note: No. 2, ahead of his own interview with director Questlove later in the episode.

films.title: Test Pattern
films.note: No. 3. Shatara Michelle Ford's first film, originally on the festival circuit in 2019 but not released to the public until February 2021. Sean calls it a tough but gracefully made watch.

films.title: No Sudden Move
films.note: No. 4. Soderbergh's HBO Max heist film. Sean was holding a slot for it before he'd even seen it.

films.title: Saint Maud
films.note: No. 5. Rose Glass's A24 Catholic horror film. Sean calls it "not an Amanda movie," but a strong debut.

segments.heading: Amanda Dobbins's Top Five Movies of 2021 So Far
segments.subject: Movies of 2021

films.title: Quo Vadis, Aida?
films.note: No. 1. Jasmila Žbanić's Bosnian War drama, about a UN translator during the Srebrenica massacre. Amanda went in with modest expectations and came out floored.

films.title: Judas and the Black Messiah
films.note: No. 2, matching Sean's No. 1.

films.title: Summer of Soul
films.note: No. 3, matching Sean's No. 2.

films.title: Bo Burnham: Inside
films.note: No. 4. The one film on Amanda's list that acknowledges the last year without ever naming the pandemic.

films.title: No Sudden Move
films.note: No. 5, matching Sean's No. 4.

films.title: Summer of Soul
films.note: His directorial debut, cut from 40 hours of unseen 1969 Harlem Cultural Festival footage he lived with for five months before editing.

films.title: Amazing Grace
films.note: His actual north star, not The Last Dance: Sydney Pollack's Aretha Franklin concert film taught him to keep the audience inside the room without leaving the footage for context.

films.title: Uncut Gems
films.note: His comparison for the discomfort of Sly and the Family Stone's There's a Riot Going On: gripping and excellent, but stressful to sit with.

films.title: Fantastic Fungi
films.note: His answer to the show's closing question: the last great thing he saw, a documentary about mushrooms he caught while quarantining on a farm.
